label: C5091017

10.1.5.1. Data Monitoring Committee This study will use an E-DMC. The E-DMC is independent of the Pfizer study team and includes only external members. The E-DMC charter describes the role of the E-DMC in more detail.

10.1.5.1. Data Monitoring Committee The E-DMC will be responsible for ongoing monitoring of the efficacy and safety of participants in the study according to the charter. The recommendations made by the E-DMC will be forwarded to the appropriate authorized Pfizer personnel for review and final decision. Pfizer will communicate such decisions, which may include summaries of aggregate analyses of endpoint events and of safety data that are not endpoints, to regulatory authorities and investigators, as appropriate.

titles.text: AN INTERVENTIONAL EFFICACY AND SAFETY, PHASE 3, DOUBLE-BLIND, 2-ARM STUDY TO INVESTIGATE ORALLY ADMINISTERED IBUZATRELVIR COMPARED WITH PLACEBO IN NON-HOSPITALIZED SYMPTOMATIC ADULT AND ADOLESCENT PARTICIPANTS WITH COVID-19 WHO ARE AT HIGH RISK OF PROGRESSING TO SEVERE ILLNESS

sources.raw_text: Study Intervention Number: PF-07817883 Study Intervention Name: Ibuzatrelvir US IND Number: 162644 EU CT Number: 2024-517727-39-00 ClinicalTrials.gov ID: NCT06679140 Pediatric Investigational Plan Number: EMA/PE/0000229195 Protocol Number: C5091017 Phase: 3 Sponsor Legal Address: Pfizer Inc. 66 Hudson Boulevard East New York, NY 10001 United States

1.1. Synopsis AN INTERVENTIONAL EFFICACY AND SAFETY, PHASE 3, DOUBLE-BLIND, 2-ARM STUDY TO INVESTIGATE ORALLY ADMINISTERED IBUZATRELVIR COMPARED WITH PLACEBO IN NON-HOSPITALIZED SYMPTOMATIC ADULT AND ADOLESCENT PARTICIPANTS WITH COVID-19 WHO ARE AT HIGH RISK OF PROGRESSING TO SEVERE ILLNESS A Study to Learn About a Study Medicine Called Ibuzatrelvir in Adult and Adolescent Patients With Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) Who Are Not Hospitalized But Are at Risk For Severe Disease Regulatory Agency Identification Number(s): US IND Number: 162644 EU Clinical Trial (CT) Number: 2024-517727-39-00 ClinicalTrials.gov ID: NCT06679140 Pediatric Investigational Plan Number: EMA/PE/0000229195 Protocol Number: C5091017 Phase: 3 Ibuzatrelvir is a potent and selective inhibitor of the severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) main protease (Mpro) with low risk for drug-drug interactions (DDIs). The purpose of the study is to evaluate the efficacy and safety of ibuzatrelvir for the treatment of non-hospitalized, symptomatic, adult and adolescent participants with COVID-19 who are at high risk of progressing to severe illness.

arms.name: Treatment arm
arms.label: Treatment arm

sources.raw_text: 4.1. Overall Design This is a Phase 3, superiority, adaptive, 2-arm, parallel group, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter study in non-hospitalized, symptomatic, adult and adolescent participants with COVID-19 who are at high risk of progressing to severe illness, to determine the efficacy, safety, and tolerability of ibuzatrelvir. Approximately 2330 eligible participants with a confirmed diagnosis of SARS-CoV-2 infection within 1 day prior to randomization and with initial onset of symptoms of COVID-19 within 5 days prior to randomization will be randomized (1:1) to receive ibuzatrelvir or placebo orally twice daily for 5 days (10 doses total). After the 5-day treatment phase, participants will be followed up until Week 24 for safety and efficacy endpoint assessments. Randomization will be stratified by geographic region (North America, Europe or Rest of World) and planned background standard of care (SoC) use at baseline (yes or no). All participants will be permitted to receive the local antiviral SoC treatment for COVID-19 either as planned background SoC or as unplanned rescue medication due to worsening COVID-19 or any other reason. Planned background SoC initiated at baseline on Day 1 (ie, at randomization, and not before) is considered to be acceptable as this is planned to treat the patient's disease (if required), upon entry into the study, prior to any assessment of efficacy, and this represents the best available therapy upon which a treatment benefit for ibuzatrelvir is being demonstrated. Addition of antiviral medication that starts after baseline is considered to be unplanned rescue medication and is handled as an intercurrent event. Worsening COVID-19 is defined as worsening of COVID-19 symptoms that are at least moderate or severe in intensity OR evidence of COVID-19 disease progression as evidenced by physical examination, laboratory tests or imaging. A single planned interim analysis assessing futility (non-binding) with a sample size reassessment will be conducted for the composite primary efficacy endpoint and reviewed by an independent external data monitoring committee (E-DMC) after approximately 70% of participants have completed the Day 34 assessments. The E-DMC will also review safety data on an ongoing basis. An adjudication committee will review whether rescue medication use was due to worsening COVID-19 and adjudicate efficacy endpoints

1.1. Synopsis Study Arm(s) Arm Title Treatment arm Placebo arm Arm Description Participants will receive 600 mg Participants will receive 0 mg BID on twice a day (BID) on Day 1 to Day 5 Day 1 to Day 5 (10 doses in total) (10 doses in total)

4.3. Justification for Dose A dosing regimen of 600 mg ibuzatrelvir twice daily administered orally for 5 days will be evaluated in this study. Dose selection for this study included consideration of all relevant available preclinical and clinical data, including repeat-dose toxicology studies, in vitro pharmacology studies, and clinical safety and PK data from Phase 1 and Phase 2b studies of ibuzatrelvir.

arms.description: Participants will receive ibuzatrelvir 600 mg twice a day (BID) on Day 1 to Day 5 (10 doses in total).

Overall Design This is a Phase 3, superiority, adaptive, 2-arm, parallel group, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter study in non-hospitalized, symptomatic, adult and adolescent participants with COVID-19 who are at high risk of progressing to severe illness, to determine the efficacy, safety, and tolerability of ibuzatrelvir. 4.1. Overall Design The total study duration is up to 24 weeks and includes a screening period up to 1 day before randomization, study intervention administration through Day 5, primary efficacy assessments through Day 28 and safety F/U through Day 34. The F/U will continue monthly from Week 8 through Week 24 with telemedicine visits at Week 8, Week 16, and Week 20 and in person visits at Week 12 and Week 24, for the assessment of secondary efficacy endpoints. The total number of visits from screening to Day 34 is 7. Screening procedures may be completed on the same calendar day as randomization and Baseline/Day 1 procedures, including the first dose of study intervention. Day 21 and Day 34 visits may be performed as telemedicine visits if the participant is asymptomatic.

Overall Design The total study duration is up to 24 weeks and includes a screening period up to 1 day before randomization, study intervention administration through Day 5, primary efficacy assessments through Day 28 and safety F/U through Day 34. The F/U will continue monthly from Week 8 through Week 24 with telemedicine visits at Week 8, Week 16, and Week 20 and in person visits at Week 12 and Week 24, for the assessment of secondary efficacy endpoints. The total number of visits from screening to Day 34 is 7. Screening procedures may be completed on the same calendar day as randomization and Baseline/Day 1 procedures, including the first dose of study intervention. Day 21 and Day 34 visits may be performed as telemedicine visits if the participant is asymptomatic.

sources.raw_text: 4.1. Overall Design This is a Phase 3, superiority, adaptive, 2-arm, parallel group, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter study in non-hospitalized, symptomatic, adult and adolescent participants with COVID-19 who are at high risk of progressing to severe illness, to determine the efficacy, safety, and tolerability of ibuzatrelvir. Approximately 2330 eligible participants with a confirmed diagnosis of SARS-CoV-2 infection within 1 day prior to randomization and with initial onset of symptoms of COVID-19 within 5 days prior to randomization will be randomized (1:1) to receive ibuzatrelvir or placebo orally twice daily for 5 days (10 doses total). After the 5-day treatment phase, participants will be followed up until Week 24 for safety and efficacy endpoint assessments. Randomization will be stratified by geographic region (North America, Europe or Rest of World) and planned background standard of care (SoC) use at baseline (yes or no). All participants will be permitted to receive the local antiviral SoC treatment for COVID-19 either as planned background SoC or as unplanned rescue medication due to worsening COVID-19 or any other reason. Planned background SoC initiated at baseline on Day 1 (ie, at randomization, and not before) is considered to be acceptable as this is planned to treat the patient's disease (if required), upon entry into the study, prior to any assessment of efficacy, and this represents the best available therapy upon which a treatment benefit for ibuzatrelvir is being demonstrated. Addition of antiviral medication that starts after baseline is considered to be unplanned rescue medication and is handled as an intercurrent event. Worsening COVID-19 is defined as worsening of COVID-19 symptoms that are at least moderate or severe in intensity OR evidence of COVID-19 disease progression as evidenced by physical examination, laboratory tests or imaging. A single planned interim analysis assessing futility (non-binding) with a sample size reassessment will be conducted for the composite primary efficacy endpoint and reviewed by an independent external data monitoring committee (E-DMC) after approximately 70% of participants have completed the Day 34 assessments. The E-DMC will also review safety data on an ongoing basis. An adjudication committee will review whether rescue medication use was due to worsening COVID-19 and adjudicate efficacy endpoints

The total number of visits from screening to Day 34 is 7. Screening procedures may be completed on the same calendar day as randomization and Baseline/Day 1 procedures, including the first dose of study intervention. Day 21 and Day 34 visits may be performed as telemedicine visits if the participant is asymptomatic.

4.3. Justification for Dose A dosing regimen of 600 mg ibuzatrelvir twice daily administered orally for 5 days will be evaluated in this study. Dose selection for this study included consideration of all relevant available preclinical and clinical data, including repeat-dose toxicology studies, in vitro pharmacology studies, and clinical safety and PK data from Phase 1 and Phase 2b studies of ibuzatrelvir.

section_paths: 9 STATISTICAL CONSIDERATIONS > 9.1 Statistical Hypothesis > 9.1.1.1 Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands

sources.section_path: 9 STATISTICAL CONSIDERATIONS > 9.1 Statistical Hypothesis > 9.1.1.1 Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands
sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.1. Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands Population: Non-hospitalized adult and adolescent patients with symptomatic COVID-19 who are at high risk of progression to severe disease and were treated ≤5 days after COVID-19 symptom onset and who were not receiving background SoC treatment for their COVID-19 infection at baseline (ie, initiated at randomization), and as defined by study inclusion/exclusion criteria included in the mITT Analysis Set. Variable: Proportion of participants with COVID-19-related emergency department visits with administration of supplemental oxygen, COVID-19 antiviral, or IV treatment (eg, hydration, antibiotics, or corticosteroids), all-cause hospitalization, or all-cause death through Day 28. Intercurrent Events: For participants who discontinue from study before Day 28 or are lost to follow-up, missing data will be imputed using multiple imputation. Treatment effect will be estimated without regard to adherence to randomized treatment. Receipt of additional antiviral therapy that is unplanned and starts after baseline is considered rescue medication and will be considered an intercurrent event. Rescue medication use due to worsening COVID-19 will be reviewed by the adjudication committee (see Section 10.1.5.2). The intercurrent event of rescue medication use will be handled as follows based on whether the rescue was given for worsening COVID-19:

sources.section_path: 9 STATISTICAL CONSIDERATIONS > 9.1 Statistical Hypothesis > 9.1.1.1 Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands
sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.1. Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands If a participant starts rescue medication and does not have a composite primary endpoint event through Day 28, and:

sources.section_path: 9 STATISTICAL CONSIDERATIONS > 9.1 Statistical Hypothesis > 9.1.1.1 Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands
sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.1. Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands o If rescue medication is administered because of worsening COVID-19, the participant will be considered as having a composite primary endpoint event with the rescue medication start date as the event date, or o If rescue medication is administered not because of worsening COVID-19, the participant’s data will be considered to be missing from the start of rescue medication onwards and will be imputed using multiple imputation.

sources.section_path: 9 STATISTICAL CONSIDERATIONS > 9.1 Statistical Hypothesis > 9.1.1.1 Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands
sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.1. Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands Population-level summary measure: Difference in the proportion of patients requiring COVID-19-related emergency department visits with administration of supplemental oxygen, COVID-19 antiviral, or IV treatment (eg, hydration, antibiotics, or corticosteroids), all-cause hospitalization, or all-cause death through Day 28, between ibuzatrelvir and placebo treatment groups. Therefore, the primary estimand is the difference in proportions of patients requiring COVID-19-related emergency department visits with administration of supplemental oxygen, COVID-19 antiviral, or IV treatment (eg, hydration, antibiotics, or corticosteroids), all-cause hospitalization, or all-cause death through Day 28 between ibuzatrelvir and placebo in non- hospitalized adult and adolescent patients with symptomatic COVID-19 who are at high risk of progression to severe disease and were treated ≤5 days after COVID-19 symptom onset, and who were not receiving background SoC treatment for their COVID-19 infection at baseline (ie, initiated at randomization), and as defined by study inclusion/exclusion criteria, included in the mITT Analysis Set. For participants who discontinue before Day 28 or are lost to follow-up, or who receive rescue medication adjudicated as not due to worsening COVID-19 and without experiencing any composite primary endpoint event through Day 28, missing data will be imputed using multiple imputation. Participants who receive rescue medication adjudicated as due to worsening COVID-19 or experience a composite primary endpoint event, will be considered as having a composite primary endpoint event. The date of this event will be the earlier of: the rescue medication start date and the actual event date. Only adjudicated data will be used for the analysis of COVID-19-related emergency department visits and rescue medication administered.

9.2. Analysis Sets For the primary efficacy endpoint analysis and key secondary efficacy endpoint analyses, the mITT and mITT1 sets will be used. The Safety Analysis Set will be used in the analyses of the safety data.

sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.1. Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands Population-level summary measure: Difference in the proportion of patients requiring COVID-19-related emergency department visits with administration of supplemental oxygen, COVID-19 antiviral, or IV treatment (eg, hydration, antibiotics, or corticosteroids), all-cause hospitalization, or all-cause death through Day 28, between ibuzatrelvir and placebo treatment groups. Therefore, the primary estimand is the difference in proportions of patients requiring COVID-19-related emergency department visits with administration of supplemental oxygen, COVID-19 antiviral, or IV treatment (eg, hydration, antibiotics, or corticosteroids), all-cause hospitalization, or all-cause death through Day 28 between ibuzatrelvir and placebo in non- hospitalized adult and adolescent patients with symptomatic COVID-19 who are at high risk of progression to severe disease and were treated ≤5 days after COVID-19 symptom onset, and who were not receiving background SoC treatment for their COVID-19 infection at baseline (ie, initiated at randomization), and as defined by study inclusion/exclusion criteria, included in the mITT Analysis Set. Participants who receive rescue medication adjudicated as due to worsening COVID-19 or experience a composite primary endpoint event, will be considered as having a composite primary endpoint event. The date of this event will be the earlier of: the rescue medication start date and the actual event date. Only adjudicated data will be used for the analysis of COVID-19-related emergency department visits and rescue medication administered.

sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.1. Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands If a participant starts rescue medication and does not have a composite primary endpoint event through Day 28, and:

sources.section_path: 9 STATISTICAL CONSIDERATIONS > 9.1 Statistical Hypothesis > 9.1.1.1 Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands
sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.1. Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands o If rescue medication is administered because of worsening COVID-19, the participant will be considered as having a composite primary endpoint event with the rescue medication start date as the event date, or o If rescue medication is administered not because of worsening COVID-19, the participant’s data will be considered to be missing from the start of rescue medication onwards and will be imputed using multiple imputation.

sources.section_path: 9 STATISTICAL CONSIDERATIONS > 9.1 Statistical Hypothesis > 9.1.1.1 Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands
sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.1. Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands Population-level summary measure: Difference in the proportion of patients requiring COVID-19-related emergency department visits with administration of supplemental oxygen, COVID-19 antiviral, or IV treatment (eg, hydration, antibiotics, or corticosteroids), all-cause hospitalization, or all-cause death through Day 28, between ibuzatrelvir and placebo treatment groups. Therefore, the primary estimand is the difference in proportions of patients requiring COVID-19-related emergency department visits with administration of supplemental oxygen, COVID-19 antiviral, or IV treatment (eg, hydration, antibiotics, or corticosteroids), all-cause hospitalization, or all-cause death through Day 28 between ibuzatrelvir and placebo in non- hospitalized adult and adolescent patients with symptomatic COVID-19 who are at high risk of progression to severe disease and were treated ≤5 days after COVID-19 symptom onset, and who were not receiving background SoC treatment for their COVID-19 infection at baseline (ie, initiated at randomization), and as defined by study inclusion/exclusion criteria, included in the mITT Analysis Set. For participants who discontinue before Day 28 or are lost to follow-up, or who receive rescue medication adjudicated as not due to worsening COVID-19 and without experiencing any composite primary endpoint event through Day 28, missing data will be imputed using multiple imputation. Participants who receive rescue medication adjudicated as due to worsening COVID-19 or experience a composite primary endpoint event, will be considered as having a composite primary endpoint event. The date of this event will be the earlier of: the rescue medication start date and the actual event date. Only adjudicated data will be used for the analysis of COVID-19-related emergency department visits and rescue medication administered.

9.2. Analysis Sets For the primary efficacy endpoint analysis and key secondary efficacy endpoint analyses, the mITT and mITT1 sets will be used. The Safety Analysis Set will be used in the analyses of the safety data.

intercurrentEvents.name: Missing data
intercurrentEvents.label: Missing data
intercurrentEvents.strategy: MI approach will be used to impute missing primary efficacy endpoint information for participants who discontinue before Day 28 assessment or are lost to follow-up prior to Day 28, or after rescue medication is administered not because of worsening COVID-19 for participants who do not have a composite primary endpoint event through Day 28. Only adjudicated data will be used for the analysis of COVID-19-related emergency department visits and rescue medication data.

sources.section_path: 9 STATISTICAL CONSIDERATIONS > 9.1 Statistical Hypothesis > 9.1.1.1 Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands
sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.1. Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands Population: Non-hospitalized adult and adolescent patients with symptomatic COVID-19 who are at high risk of progression to severe disease and were treated ≤5 days after COVID-19 symptom onset and who were not receiving background SoC treatment for their COVID-19 infection at baseline (ie, initiated at randomization), and as defined by study inclusion/exclusion criteria included in the mITT Analysis Set. Variable: Proportion of participants with COVID-19-related emergency department visits with administration of supplemental oxygen, COVID-19 antiviral, or IV treatment (eg, hydration, antibiotics, or corticosteroids), all-cause hospitalization, or all-cause death through Day 28. Intercurrent Events: For participants who discontinue from study before Day 28 or are lost to follow-up, missing data will be imputed using multiple imputation. Treatment effect will be estimated without regard to adherence to randomized treatment. Receipt of additional antiviral therapy that is unplanned and starts after baseline is considered rescue medication and will be considered an intercurrent event. Rescue medication use due to worsening COVID-19 will be reviewed by the adjudication committee (see Section 10.1.5.2). The intercurrent event of rescue medication use will be handled as follows based on whether the rescue was given for worsening COVID-19:

sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.2. Key Secondary Estimands Population-level summary measure: Difference in the proportion of patients with PACE (see Section 8.2.3) from Day 29 to Week 24 between ibuzatrelvir and placebo treatment groups. Therefore, the third key secondary estimand is the difference in proportions of patients with PACE (see Section 8.2.3) from Day 29 to Week 24 between ibuzatrelvir and placebo in non- hospitalized adult and adolescent patients with symptomatic COVID-19 who are at high risk of progression to severe disease and were treated ≤5 days after COVID-19 symptom onset, and who were not receiving background SoC treatment for their COVID-19 infection at baseline (ie, initiated at randomization), and as defined by study inclusion/exclusion criteria, included in the mITT Analysis Set. For participants who discontinue from study before Week 24 or are lost to follow-up, missing data will be imputed using multiple imputation or other appropriate imputation methods which will be described in the SAP.

sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.2. Key Secondary Estimands Intercurrent Events: For participants who discontinued from the study, if their last Long COVID/PASC PRO was completed at Week 12 or later, then the last completed assessment will be utilized in the analysis. However, if the last completed assessment was prior to Week 12, the participant’s Long COVID/PASC PRO assessment will not be utilized and will be considered missing and will not be imputed. This will be estimated without regard to adherence to randomized treatment. Population-level summary measure: Difference in the proportion of patients with Long COVID/PASC symptoms at Week 24, based on Long COVID/PASC PRO between ibuzatrelvir and placebo treatment groups. Therefore, this fourth key secondary estimand is the difference in proportions of patients with Long COVID/PASC symptoms at Week 24, based on Long COVID/PASC PRO between ibuzatrelvir and placebo in non-hospitalized adult and adolescent patients with symptomatic COVID-19 who are at high risk of progression to severe disease and were treated ≤5 days after COVID-19 symptom onset, and who were not receiving background SoC treatment for their COVID-19 infection at baseline (ie, initiated at randomization), and as defined by study inclusion/exclusion criteria, included in the mITT Analysis Set. For the intercurrent event of discontinuation from the study, only Long COVID/PASC PROs completed at Week 12 or later will be utilized in the analysis.

Key Secondary Estimands The first key secondary estimand is defined by the following attributes: Population: Non-hospitalized adult and adolescent patients with symptomatic COVID-19 who are at high risk of progression to severe disease and were treated, ≤5 days after COVID-19 symptom onset, and who were not receiving background SoC treatment for their COVID-19 infection at baseline (ie, initiated at randomization), and as defined by study inclusion/exclusion criteria, included in the mITT Analysis Set.

sources.section_path: 9 STATISTICAL CONSIDERATIONS > 9.1 Statistical Hypothesis > 9.1.1.2 Key Secondary Estimands
sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.2. Key Secondary Estimands Variable: Time (days) to sustained (assessed for 2 consecutive days) resolution of all targeted COVID-19 symptoms, through Day 28.

sources.section_path: 9 STATISTICAL CONSIDERATIONS > 9.1 Statistical Hypothesis > 9.1.1.2 Key Secondary Estimands
sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.2. Key Secondary Estimands Intercurrent Events: For participants who discontinue before Day 28 or are lost to follow-up, missing data will be imputed using multiple imputation. Treatment effect will be estimated without regard to adherence to randomized treatment. For a participant who discontinues from the study before Day 28 without symptom resolution, the censoring date will be at the last date on which symptom resolution is assessed or Day 27 whichever occurs first. Participants with a composite primary endpoint event, or who started rescue medication because of worsening COVID-19, through Day 28 will be classified as not achieving symptom resolution and will be censored at Day 27 (as that will be the last day that symptom resolution for 2 consecutive days can be achieved).

For a participant who discontinues from the study before Day 28 without symptom resolution, the censoring date will be at the last date on which symptom resolution is assessed or Day 27 whichever occurs first. Participants with a composite primary endpoint event, or who started rescue medication through Day 28 will be classified as not achieving symptom resolution and will be censored at Day 27.

notes.text: Complete examination to be performed at screening.
All subsequent examinations may be targeted, or symptoms driven.
Examinations may be performed at the discretion of the investigator at any visit, if required, until Day 34.
See Section 8.3.1 and Section 8.3.2.
At screening, the complete physical examination must include, at a minimum, assessments of the cardiovascular, respiratory, gastrointestinal, and neurological systems.
Height and weight will also be measured and recorded at screening.
Investigators should pay special attention to clinical signs related to previous serious illnesses.
Physical examination findings collected during the study will be considered source record and will not be required to be reported, unless otherwise noted.
Any untoward physical examination findings identified during the active collection period that meet the definition of an AE or SAE must be reported according to Sections 8.4.1 to 8.4.3.

sources.section_path: 8.3 Safety Assessments > 8.3.3 Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments
sources.raw_text: 8.3.3. Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments Laboratory safety parameters will be graded according to the DAIDS Table for Grading the Severity of Adult and Pediatric Adverse Events, version 2.1 [11]. See Appendix 2 for the list of clinical safety laboratory tests to be performed and the SoA for the timing and frequency. All protocol-required laboratory assessments, as defined in Appendix 2, must be conducted in accordance with the laboratory manual and the SoA. Unscheduled clinical laboratory measurements may be obtained at any time during the study to assess any perceived safety issues. The investigator must review the laboratory report, document this review, and record any clinically significant changes occurring during the study in the AE section of the CRF. Clinically significant abnormal laboratory test findings are those that are not associated with the underlying disease, unless judged by the investigator to be more severe than expected for the participant's condition.

notes.text: At screening, laboratory assessments are not required unless deemed necessary by the investigator to confirm eligibility.
If screening laboratory assessments are deemed necessary, they will be performed at the local laboratory.
Safety labs, including hematology and blood chemistry, may be performed at the discretion of the investigator at any visit, if required, until Day 34.
Baseline laboratory assessments should be collected prior to first dose of study intervention.
Abnormal laboratory values related to AEs should be followed until resolution or when the AE is stable or otherwise explained or no longer considered clinically significant.
See Section 8.3.3 and Appendix 2.
Protocol-required hematology assessments in Appendix 2 are hematocrit, hemoglobin, platelet count, WBC count, total neutrophils (Abs), and total lymphocytes (Abs).
Laboratory safety parameters will be graded according to the DAIDS Table for Grading the Severity of Adult and Pediatric Adverse Events, version 2.1.
All protocol-required laboratory assessments must be conducted in accordance with the laboratory manual and the SoA.
Unscheduled clinical laboratory measurements may be obtained at any time during the study to assess any perceived safety issues.
The investigator must review the laboratory report, document this review, and record any clinically significant changes occurring during the study in the AE section of the CRF.

All laboratory tests with values considered clinically significant and abnormal during participation in the study or within 28 days after the last dose of study intervention should be repeated until the values return to normal or baseline or are no longer considered clinically significant by the investigator or study medical monitor.
Table 4 reflex testing: If Hb/RBC abnormal, perform MCV, MCH, MCHC, neutrophils (%), eosinophils (%), basophils (%), lymphocytes (%), monocytes (%), and RBC distribution width.
The investigator must review the laboratory report, document this review, and record any clinically relevant changes occurring during the study in the AE section of the CRF.

All laboratory tests with values considered clinically significant and abnormal during participation in the study or within 28 days after the last dose of study intervention should be repeated until the values return to normal or baseline or are no longer considered clinically significant by the investigator or study medical monitor.
For suspected DILI: AST, ALT, T bili, albumin, CK, direct and indirect bilirubin, GGT, PT/INR, eosinophils (%), alkaline phosphatase.
Additional testing that may be warranted for suspected DILI: acetaminophen/paracetamol or protein adduct levels, hepatitis serology (even if screening negative), total bile acids, liver imaging.
For suspected DICI/DIKI: Screat, Scys, eGFR (Screat only and combined Screat+Scys), eCrCl, UACR, urinalysis.
The investigator must review the laboratory report, document this review, and record any clinically relevant changes occurring during the study in the AE section of the CRF.

sources.raw_text: 8.3.3. Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments Laboratory safety parameters will be graded according to the DAIDS Table for Grading the Severity of Adult and Pediatric Adverse Events, version 2.1 [11]. See Appendix 2 for the list of clinical safety laboratory tests to be performed and the SoA for the timing and frequency. All protocol-required laboratory assessments, as defined in Appendix 2, must be conducted in accordance with the laboratory manual and the SoA. Unscheduled clinical laboratory measurements may be obtained at any time during the study to assess any perceived safety issues. The investigator must review the laboratory report, document this review, and record any clinically significant changes occurring during the study in the AE section of the CRF. Clinically significant abnormal laboratory test findings are those that are not associated with the underlying disease, unless judged by the investigator to be more severe than expected for the participant's condition.

notes.text: WOCBP only at screening.
A negative urine or serum (β-hCG) pregnancy test must be confirmed at screening for WOCBP only.
Pregnancy tests will also be done whenever 1 menstrual cycle is missed during the active treatment period, or when a potential pregnancy is otherwise suspected.
Pregnancy test is done at Day 10 or ET visit, if this occurs before the Day 34 visit.
If Day 34 is a telemedicine visit, a pregnancy test may be done at home.
See Section 8.3.4 and Section 8.3.4.1.
WOCBP.
A urine or serum pregnancy test is required at screening.
Following screening, pregnancy tests may be urine or serum tests and must have a sensitivity of at least 25 mIU/mL.

Following a negative pregnancy test result at screening and provided that appropriate contraception is already in place, a second negative pregnancy test result is required at the baseline visit if screening and baseline do not happen on the same day, prior to starting the study intervention.
Pregnancy tests will also be done whenever 1 menstrual cycle is missed during the active treatment period or when potential pregnancy is otherwise suspected.
Pregnancy tests may also be repeated if requested by IRBs/ECs or if required by local regulations.
If a urine test cannot be confirmed as negative, a serum pregnancy test is required; the participant must be excluded if the serum pregnancy result is positive.

A home urine pregnancy testing kit with sensitivity of at least 25 mIU/mL may be used if compliant with local regulatory requirements; the outcome should be documented in the source documents/medical records and relevant data recorded on the CRF, and adherence to protocol-required contraception should be confirmed.

See Section 8.7. Retained Research X Collect in adult participants only (≥18 years of age at screening). sources.raw_text: 1.3. Schedule of Activities Visit Identifier Screen. Treatment F/U ET Notes Abbreviations used in this Period table may be found in Section 10.15 Screening procedures may be completed on the same calendar day as randomization Visit Window e n i l e s a B ) 1 y a D ( 5 y a D 0 1 y a D 4 1 y a D 1 2 y a D 4 3 y a D o t r o i r p ( ) 4 3 y a D and Baseline/Day 1 procedures, including first dose of study intervention. If screening and baseline visits are conducted on the same calendar day, assessments and procedures that are listed to occur at both visits only need to be completed once. Day relative to start of study intervention (Day 1). Day 1, Day 5, Day 10, Day 14, and ET visits are mandatory in person visits. Day -1 to Day 1 0 days (± 1 day) (±1 day) (±2 days) (±2 days) (±3 days) Day 21 and Day 34 visits may be performed as telemedicine visits, if the participant is asymptomatic. For assessments/procedures during the F/U period from Week 8 through Week 24 see Table 2. COVID-19 Rapid antigen X RAT to be performed at screening to confirm eligibility. Investigator sites will use test testing kits that are authorized for use in this study. See Section 8.7.2. If RAT at screening is negative and there is strong suspicion of the participant having COVID-19, and if symptoms persist or worsen, or new symptoms appear, the RAT may be repeated the following day, provided all other eligibility criteria are still met. SARS-CoV-2 RNA level X X X X X Day 1: NP sample must be collected prior to first dose of study intervention. assessment (NP) Day 3: A nasal swab will be self-collected by the participant at home and either returned to the site at Day 5 or shipped to central lab via the courier. In addition to the required sample collections indicated, from Day 5 to Day 34 an NP swab for viral RNA sample should be collected within 48 hours of COVID-19 symptom onset if a participant experiences moderate or severe symptom rebound. Collect at an unscheduled visit if there is no scheduled visit within 48 hours. See Section 10.12. In addition to the time points indicated, NP sample may be collected at Day 21 and Day 34 if the participant presents with new onset of COVID-19 symptoms after prior resolution, or if symptoms persist from baseline visit. See Section 8.7.3. Biomarker blood X X X X In adult participants only (≥18 years of age at screening), a biomarker blood sample sampling will also be collected at Day 21 and Day 34, or at ET along with NP swabs, if the participant presents with new onset of COVID-19 symptoms after prior resolution, or if symptoms persist from baseline visit. See Section 8.7. Retained Research X Collect in adult participants only (≥18 years of age at screening). Sample for Genetics (Prep If not collected on the designated collection day, collect at the next available time point D1) when biospecimens are being collected in conjunction with a participant visit. See Section 8.6.2.

PROMIS instrument anchors (Week 4) through Week 24 Global impression questions and PROMIS instruments will be answered weekly from Day 29 (Week 4) through Week 24 after Long COVID/PASC symptom PRO is completed. Only adult participants (≥18 years of age at screening) will be asked to complete the global impression questions for Long COVID/PASC. See Section 8.2.5.5. Participant-completed study diary (WPAI) X X Only adult participants (≥18 years of age at screening) will be asked to complete the WPAI. WPAI will be answered at Day 29, Week 12 and Week 24 after Long COVID/PASC symptom PRO is completed. See Section 8.2.5.3. 1.3. Schedule of Activities Visit Identifier Screen. Treatment F/U ET Notes Abbreviations used in this Period table may be found in Section 10.15 Screening procedures may be completed on the same calendar day as randomization Visit Window e n i l e s a B ) 1 y a D ( 5 y a D 0 1 y a D 4 1 y a D 1 2 y a D 4 3 y a D o t r o i r p ( ) 4 3 y a D and Baseline/Day 1 procedures, including first dose of study intervention. If screening and baseline visits are conducted on the same calendar day, assessments and procedures that are listed to occur at both visits only need to be completed once. Day relative to start of study intervention (Day 1). Day 1, Day 5, Day 10, Day 14, and ET visits are mandatory in person visits. Day -1 to Day 1 0 days (± 1 day) (±1 day) (±2 days) (±2 days) (±3 days) Day 21 and Day 34 visits may be performed as telemedicine visits, if the participant is asymptomatic. For assessments/procedures during the F/U period from Week 8 through Week 24 see Table 2. Hematology X X Screening visit: Laboratory assessments are not required at screening unless deemed necessary by the investigator to confirm eligibility. If deemed necessary, laboratory assessments at screening will be performed at the local laboratory. Safety labs, including hematology and blood chemistry may be performed, at the discretion of the investigator at any visit, if required, until Day 34. Blood chemistry X X Baseline laboratory assessments should be collected prior to first dose of study intervention. Abnormal laboratory values related to AEs should be followed until resolution or when the AE is stable or otherwise explained or no longer considered clinically significant. See Section 8.3.3 and Appendix 2. Pregnancy test X X X X X A negative urine or serum (β-hCG) pregnancy test must be confirmed at screening for WOCBP only. Pregnancy tests will also be done whenever 1 menstrual cycle is missed during the active treatment period (or when a potential pregnancy is otherwise suspected) and at Day 10 or ET visit, if this occurs before the Day 34 visit. See Section 8.3.4. If Day 34 is a telemedicine visit, a pregnancy test may be done at home. See Section 8.3.4.1. FSH X FSH testing is to be performed locally when required to confirm postmenopausal status. A participant may be enrolled in the study prior to the test result being available as long as the FSH test result confirms postmenopausal status prior to dosing. See Appendix 2 and Appendix 4. SARS-CoV-2 Serology X X See Section 10.2 BIOMARKER See laboratory manual. ASSESSMENTS

Treatment F/U ET Notes Abbreviations used in this Period table may be found in Section 10.15 Screening procedures may be completed on the same calendar day as randomization Visit Window e n i l e s a B ) 1 y a D ( 5 y a D 0 1 y a D 4 1 y a D 1 2 y a D 4 3 y a D o t r o i r p ( ) 4 3 y a D and Baseline/Day 1 procedures, including first dose of study intervention. If screening and baseline visits are conducted on the same calendar day, assessments and procedures that are listed to occur at both visits only need to be completed once. Day relative to start of study intervention (Day 1). Day 1, Day 5, Day 10, Day 14, and ET visits are mandatory in person visits. Day -1 to Day 1 0 days (± 1 day) (±1 day) (±2 days) (±2 days) (±3 days) Day 21 and Day 34 visits may be performed as telemedicine visits, if the participant is asymptomatic. For assessments/procedures during the F/U period from Week 8 through Week 24 see Table 2. Hematology X X Screening visit: Laboratory assessments are not required at screening unless deemed necessary by the investigator to confirm eligibility. If deemed necessary, laboratory assessments at screening will be performed at the local laboratory. Safety labs, including hematology and blood chemistry may be performed, at the discretion of the investigator at any visit, if required, until Day 34. Blood chemistry X X Baseline laboratory assessments should be collected prior to first dose of study intervention. Abnormal laboratory values related to AEs should be followed until resolution or when the AE is stable or otherwise explained or no longer considered clinically significant. See Section 8.3.3 and Appendix 2. Pregnancy test X X X X X A negative urine or serum (β-hCG) pregnancy test must be confirmed at screening for WOCBP only. Pregnancy tests will also be done whenever 1 menstrual cycle is missed during the active treatment period (or when a potential pregnancy is otherwise suspected) and at Day 10 or ET visit, if this occurs before the Day 34 visit. See Section 8.3.4. If Day 34 is a telemedicine visit, a pregnancy test may be done at home. See Section 8.3.4.1. FSH X FSH testing is to be performed locally when required to confirm postmenopausal status. A participant may be enrolled in the study prior to the test result being available as long as the FSH test result confirms postmenopausal status prior to dosing. See Appendix 2 and Appendix 4. SARS-CoV-2 Serology X X See Section 10.2 BIOMARKER See laboratory manual. ASSESSMENTS

If screening and baseline visits are conducted on the same calendar day, assessments and procedures that are listed to occur at both visits only need to be completed once. Day relative to start of study intervention (Day 1). Day 1, Day 5, Day 10, Day 14, and ET visits are mandatory in person visits. Day -1 to Day 1 0 days (± 1 day) (±1 day) (±2 days) (±2 days) (±3 days) Day 21 and Day 34 visits may be performed as telemedicine visits, if the participant is asymptomatic. For assessments/procedures during the F/U period from Week 8 through Week 24 see Table 2. Hematology X X Screening visit: Laboratory assessments are not required at screening unless deemed necessary by the investigator to confirm eligibility. If deemed necessary, laboratory assessments at screening will be performed at the local laboratory. Safety labs, including hematology and blood chemistry may be performed, at the discretion of the investigator at any visit, if required, until Day 34. Blood chemistry X X Baseline laboratory assessments should be collected prior to first dose of study intervention. Abnormal laboratory values related to AEs should be followed until resolution or when the AE is stable or otherwise explained or no longer considered clinically significant. See Section 8.3.3 and Appendix 2. Pregnancy test X X X X X A negative urine or serum (β-hCG) pregnancy test must be confirmed at screening for WOCBP only. Pregnancy tests will also be done whenever 1 menstrual cycle is missed during the active treatment period (or when a potential pregnancy is otherwise suspected) and at Day 10 or ET visit, if this occurs before the Day 34 visit. See Section 8.3.4. If Day 34 is a telemedicine visit, a pregnancy test may be done at home. See Section 8.3.4.1. FSH X FSH testing is to be performed locally when required to confirm postmenopausal status. A participant may be enrolled in the study prior to the test result being available as long as the FSH test result confirms postmenopausal status prior to dosing. See Appendix 2 and Appendix 4. SARS-CoV-2 Serology X X See Section 10.2 BIOMARKER See laboratory manual. ASSESSMENTS

notes.text: Vital signs may be taken at the discretion of the investigator at any visit, if required, until Day 34.
See Section 8.3.1 and Section 8.3.2.

Blood pressure and pulse rate measurements will be assessed with the participant in the supine or seated position with their feet on the floor when possible with a completely automated device; manual techniques will be used only if an automated device is not available.
Blood pressure and pulse rate measurements should be preceded by at least 5 minutes of rest in a quiet setting without distractions.
Blood pressure and pulse rate will be taken before collection for laboratory tests and consist of a single blood pressure measurement and single pulse rate measurement.
Temperature and respiratory rate will be assessed.
Oxygen saturation level will be assessed as part of the vital signs assessment.
Any untoward vital sign findings identified during the active collection period that meet the definition of an AE or SAE must be reported according to Sections 8.4.1 to 8.4.3.

sources.section_path: 8.3 Safety Assessments > 8.3.3 Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments
sources.raw_text: 8.3.3. Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments Laboratory safety parameters will be graded according to the DAIDS Table for Grading the Severity of Adult and Pediatric Adverse Events, version 2.1 [11]. See Appendix 2 for the list of clinical safety laboratory tests to be performed and the SoA for the timing and frequency. All protocol-required laboratory assessments, as defined in Appendix 2, must be conducted in accordance with the laboratory manual and the SoA. Unscheduled clinical laboratory measurements may be obtained at any time during the study to assess any perceived safety issues. The investigator must review the laboratory report, document this review, and record any clinically significant changes occurring during the study in the AE section of the CRF. Clinically significant abnormal laboratory test findings are those that are not associated with the underlying disease, unless judged by the investigator to be more severe than expected for the participant's condition.

sources.section_path: 8.3 Safety Assessments > 8.3.3 Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments
sources.raw_text: 8.3.3. Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments Laboratory safety parameters will be graded according to the DAIDS Table for Grading the Severity of Adult and Pediatric Adverse Events, version 2.1 [11]. See Appendix 2 for the list of clinical safety laboratory tests to be performed and the SoA for the timing and frequency. All protocol-required laboratory assessments, as defined in Appendix 2, must be conducted in accordance with the laboratory manual and the SoA. Unscheduled clinical laboratory measurements may be obtained at any time during the study to assess any perceived safety issues. The investigator must review the laboratory report, document this review, and record any clinically significant changes occurring during the study in the AE section of the CRF. Clinically significant abnormal laboratory test findings are those that are not associated with the underlying disease, unless judged by the investigator to be more severe than expected for the participant's condition.

sources.section_path: 8.3 Safety Assessments > 8.3.3 Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments
sources.raw_text: 8.3.3. Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments Unscheduled clinical laboratory measurements may be obtained at any time during the study to assess any perceived safety issues. The investigator must review the laboratory report, document this review, and record any clinically significant changes occurring during the study in the AE section of the CRF. Clinically significant abnormal laboratory test findings are those that are not associated with the underlying disease, unless judged by the investigator to be more severe than expected for the participant's condition. All laboratory tests with values considered clinically significant and abnormal during participation in the study or within 28 days after the last dose of study intervention should be repeated until the values return to normal or baseline or are no longer considered clinically significant by the investigator or study medical monitor.

10.2. Appendix 2: Clinical Laboratory Tests Hematology Chemistry Other Hematocrit Urea/BUN At screening: Hemoglobin Creatinine (eGFR/CrCl) FSHb Platelet count Scysa (at screening or baseline) Pregnancy test (β-hCG)c WBC count Sodium Total neutrophils (Abs) Potassium At baseline, D14, Week 12 and Total lymphocytes (Abs) AST, ALT Week 24: Total bilirubin SARS-CoV-2 serology (anti S Alkaline phosphatase IgG+IgM quantitative; anti N IgG+IgM qualitative)

sources.section_path: 8.3 Safety Assessments > 8.3.3 Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments
sources.raw_text: 8.3.3. Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments Laboratory safety parameters will be graded according to the DAIDS Table for Grading the Severity of Adult and Pediatric Adverse Events, version 2.1 [11]. See Appendix 2 for the list of clinical safety laboratory tests to be performed and the SoA for the timing and frequency. All protocol-required laboratory assessments, as defined in Appendix 2, must be conducted in accordance with the laboratory manual and the SoA. Unscheduled clinical laboratory measurements may be obtained at any time during the study to assess any perceived safety issues. The investigator must review the laboratory report, document this review, and record any clinically significant changes occurring during the study in the AE section of the CRF. Clinically significant abnormal laboratory test findings are those that are not associated with the underlying disease, unless judged by the investigator to be more severe than expected for the participant's condition.

sources.section_path: 8.3 Safety Assessments > 8.3.3 Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments
sources.raw_text: 8.3.3. Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments Unscheduled clinical laboratory measurements may be obtained at any time during the study to assess any perceived safety issues. The investigator must review the laboratory report, document this review, and record any clinically significant changes occurring during the study in the AE section of the CRF. Clinically significant abnormal laboratory test findings are those that are not associated with the underlying disease, unless judged by the investigator to be more severe than expected for the participant's condition. All laboratory tests with values considered clinically significant and abnormal during participation in the study or within 28 days after the last dose of study intervention should be repeated until the values return to normal or baseline or are no longer considered clinically significant by the investigator or study medical monitor.

10.2. FSH X FSH testing is to be performed locally when required to confirm postmenopausal status. sources.section_path: 8.3 Safety Assessments > 8.3.3 Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments
sources.raw_text: 8.3.3. Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments Laboratory safety parameters will be graded according to the DAIDS Table for Grading the Severity of Adult and Pediatric Adverse Events, version 2.1 [11]. See Appendix 2 for the list of clinical safety laboratory tests to be performed and the SoA for the timing and frequency. All protocol-required laboratory assessments, as defined in Appendix 2, must be conducted in accordance with the laboratory manual and the SoA. Unscheduled clinical laboratory measurements may be obtained at any time during the study to assess any perceived safety issues. The investigator must review the laboratory report, document this review, and record any clinically significant changes occurring during the study in the AE section of the CRF. Clinically significant abnormal laboratory test findings are those that are not associated with the underlying disease, unless judged by the investigator to be more severe than expected for the participant's condition.

sources.section_path: 8.3 Safety Assessments > 8.3.3 Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments
sources.raw_text: 8.3.3. Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments Unscheduled clinical laboratory measurements may be obtained at any time during the study to assess any perceived safety issues. The investigator must review the laboratory report, document this review, and record any clinically significant changes occurring during the study in the AE section of the CRF. Clinically significant abnormal laboratory test findings are those that are not associated with the underlying disease, unless judged by the investigator to be more severe than expected for the participant's condition. All laboratory tests with values considered clinically significant and abnormal during participation in the study or within 28 days after the last dose of study intervention should be repeated until the values return to normal or baseline or are no longer considered clinically significant by the investigator or study medical monitor.

In addition to the required sample collections indicated, from Day 5 to Day 34 an NP swab for viral RNA sample should be collected within 48 hours of COVID-19 symptom onset if a participant experiences moderate or severe symptom rebound. Collect at an unscheduled visit if there is no scheduled visit within 48 hours. See Section 10.12. In addition to the time points indicated, NP sample may be collected at Day 21 and Day 34 if the participant presents with new onset of COVID-19 symptoms after prior resolution, or if symptoms persist from baseline visit. See Section 8.7.3. Sample for Genetics (Prep If not collected on the designated collection day, collect at the next available time point D1) when biospecimens are being collected in conjunction with a participant visit. See Section 8.6.2.

sources.section_path: 8.3 Safety Assessments > 8.3.3 Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments
sources.raw_text: 8.3.3. Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments Unscheduled clinical laboratory measurements may be obtained at any time during the study to assess any perceived safety issues. The investigator must review the laboratory report, document this review, and record any clinically significant changes occurring during the study in the AE section of the CRF. Clinically significant abnormal laboratory test findings are those that are not associated with the underlying disease, unless judged by the investigator to be more severe than expected for the participant's condition. All laboratory tests with values considered clinically significant and abnormal during participation in the study or within 28 days after the last dose of study intervention should be repeated until the values return to normal or baseline or are no longer considered clinically significant by the investigator or study medical monitor.

10.2. Appendix 2: Clinical Laboratory Tests Hematology Chemistry Other Hematocrit Urea/BUN At screening: Hemoglobin Creatinine (eGFR/CrCl) FSHb Platelet count Scysa (at screening or baseline) Pregnancy test (β-hCG)c WBC count Sodium Total neutrophils (Abs) Potassium At baseline, D14, Week 12 and Total lymphocytes (Abs) AST, ALT Week 24: Total bilirubin SARS-CoV-2 serology (anti S Alkaline phosphatase IgG+IgM quantitative; anti N IgG+IgM qualitative)

Hematology X X Screening visit: Laboratory assessments are not required at screening unless deemed necessary by the investigator to confirm eligibility. Blood chemistry X X Baseline laboratory assessments should be collected prior to first dose of study intervention. See Section 8.3.3 and Appendix 2. Pregnancy test X X X X X A negative urine or serum (β-hCG) pregnancy test must be confirmed at screening for WOCBP only. Pregnancy tests will also be done whenever 1 menstrual cycle is missed during the active treatment period (or when a potential pregnancy is otherwise suspected) and at Day 10 or ET visit, if this occurs before the Day 34 visit. See Section 8.3.4. If Day 34 is a telemedicine visit, a pregnancy test may be done at home. See Section 8.3.4.1. FSH X FSH testing is to be performed locally when required to confirm postmenopausal status. A participant may be enrolled in the study prior to the test result being available as long as the FSH test result confirms postmenopausal status prior to dosing. See Appendix 2 and Appendix 4. sources.section_path: 8.3 Safety Assessments > 8.3.3 Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments
sources.raw_text: 8.3.3. Clinical Safety Laboratory Assessments Unscheduled clinical laboratory measurements may be obtained at any time during the study to assess any perceived safety issues. The investigator must review the laboratory report, document this review, and record any clinically significant changes occurring during the study in the AE section of the CRF. Clinically significant abnormal laboratory test findings are those that are not associated with the underlying disease, unless judged by the investigator to be more severe than expected for the participant's condition. All laboratory tests with values considered clinically significant and abnormal during participation in the study or within 28 days after the last dose of study intervention should be repeated until the values return to normal or baseline or are no longer considered clinically significant by the investigator or study medical monitor.

sources.section_path: 9 STATISTICAL CONSIDERATIONS > 9.1 Statistical Hypothesis > 9.1.1.1 Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands
sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.1. Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands Population: Non-hospitalized adult and adolescent patients with symptomatic COVID-19 who are at high risk of progression to severe disease and were treated ≤5 days after COVID-19 symptom onset and who were not receiving background SoC treatment for their COVID-19 infection at baseline (ie, initiated at randomization), and as defined by study inclusion/exclusion criteria included in the mITT Analysis Set. Variable: Proportion of participants with COVID-19-related emergency department visits with administration of supplemental oxygen, COVID-19 antiviral, or IV treatment (eg, hydration, antibiotics, or corticosteroids), all-cause hospitalization, or all-cause death through Day 28. Intercurrent Events: For participants who discontinue from study before Day 28 or are lost to follow-up, missing data will be imputed using multiple imputation. Treatment effect will be estimated without regard to adherence to randomized treatment. Receipt of additional antiviral therapy that is unplanned and starts after baseline is considered rescue medication and will be considered an intercurrent event. Rescue medication use due to worsening COVID-19 will be reviewed by the adjudication committee (see Section 10.1.5.2). The intercurrent event of rescue medication use will be handled as follows based on whether the rescue was given for worsening COVID-19:

sources.section_path: 9 STATISTICAL CONSIDERATIONS > 9.1 Statistical Hypothesis > 9.1.1.1 Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands
sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.1. Primary Estimand/Coprimary Estimands Population-level summary measure: Difference in the proportion of patients requiring COVID-19-related emergency department visits with administration of supplemental oxygen, COVID-19 antiviral, or IV treatment (eg, hydration, antibiotics, or corticosteroids), all-cause hospitalization, or all-cause death through Day 28, between ibuzatrelvir and placebo treatment groups. Therefore, the primary estimand is the difference in proportions of patients requiring COVID-19-related emergency department visits with administration of supplemental oxygen, COVID-19 antiviral, or IV treatment (eg, hydration, antibiotics, or corticosteroids), all-cause hospitalization, or all-cause death through Day 28 between ibuzatrelvir and placebo in non- hospitalized adult and adolescent patients with symptomatic COVID-19 who are at high risk of progression to severe disease and were treated ≤5 days after COVID-19 symptom onset, and who were not receiving background SoC treatment for their COVID-19 infection at baseline (ie, initiated at randomization), and as defined by study inclusion/exclusion criteria, included in the mITT Analysis Set. For participants who discontinue before Day 28 or are lost to follow-up, or who receive rescue medication adjudicated as not due to worsening COVID-19 and without experiencing any composite primary endpoint event through Day 28, missing data will be imputed using multiple imputation. Participants who receive rescue medication adjudicated as due to worsening COVID-19 or experience a composite primary endpoint event, will be considered as having a composite primary endpoint event. The date of this event will be the earlier of: the rescue medication start date and the actual event date. Only adjudicated data will be used for the analysis of COVID-19-related emergency department visits and rescue medication administered.

sources.section_path: 9 STATISTICAL CONSIDERATIONS > 9.3 Statistical Analyses > 9.3.3 Key Secondary Efficacy Endpoint(s)/Estimand(s) Analysis
sources.raw_text: 9.3.3. Key Secondary Efficacy Endpoint(s)/Estimand(s) Analysis Time (days) to sustained resolution of all targeted COVID-19 symptoms, as assessed over 2 consecutive days, through Day 28. Resolution of all targeted COVID-19 symptoms is defined as the event occurring on the first of two consecutive days when all symptoms are scored as absent. Day 27 is the last possible day the symptom resolution can be achieved. For a participant that either completes Day 28 of the study or discontinues from the study before Day 28 without symptom resolution (censored), the censoring date will be at the last date on which symptom resolution is assessed, and time will be calculated as (Censoring Date) – (First Dose Date) +1, or Day 27, whichever occurs first. For missing symptom diary data, MI will be considered when assessing the imputation of the missing diary entries, prior to derivation of the Time to Sustained Resolution endpoint.

sources.raw_text: 9.3.4. Secondary Endpoint(s)/Estimand(s) Analysis Time (days) to sustained alleviation of all targeted COVID-19 symptoms, as assessed over 2 consecutive days, through Day 28. Alleviation of all targeted COVID-19 symptoms is defined as the event occurring on the first of two consecutive days when all symptoms scored as moderate or severe at study entry are scored as mild or absent AND all symptoms scored mild or absent at study entry are scored as absent. The first day of the 2 consecutive-day period will be considered the First Event Date. o For a participant that either completes Day 28 of the study or discontinues from the study before Day 28 without symptom alleviation (censored), the censoring date will be at the last date on which symptom alleviation is assessed, and time will be calculated as (Censoring Date) – (First Dose Date) +1, or Day 27, whichever occurs first. o For missing symptom diary data, multiple imputation techniques, such as MI will be considered when assessing the imputation of the missing diary entries, prior to derivation of the Time to Sustained Alleviation endpoint. Duration of each targeted COVID-19 symptom is defined as (First Date when the symptom achieved sustained alleviation/resolution)-(First Dose Date) + 1 for each

9.2. Analysis Sets Defined Analysis Set Description Modified Intent-To-Treat All participants randomly assigned to study intervention, (mITT) who take at least 1 dose of study intervention, were treated ≤5 days after COVID-19 symptom onset and who were not receiving background SoC treatment for their COVID-19 infection at baseline (ie, initiated at randomization). Participants will be analyzed according to the study intervention to which they were randomized. Modified Intent-To-Treat 1 All participants randomly assigned to study intervention, (mITT1) who take at least 1 dose of study intervention, and were treated ≤5 days after COVID-19 symptom onset. Participants will be analyzed according to the study intervention to which they were randomized.

sources.raw_text: 9.1.1.2. Key Secondary Estimands Intercurrent Events: For participants who discontinued from the study, if their last Long COVID/PASC PRO was completed at Week 12 or later, then the last completed assessment will be utilized in the analysis. However, if the last completed assessment was prior to Week 12, the participant’s Long COVID/PASC PRO assessment will not be utilized and will be considered missing and will not be imputed. This will be estimated without regard to adherence to randomized treatment. Population-level summary measure: Difference in the proportion of patients with Long COVID/PASC symptoms at Week 24, based on Long COVID/PASC PRO between ibuzatrelvir and placebo treatment groups. Therefore, this fourth key secondary estimand is the difference in proportions of patients with Long COVID/PASC symptoms at Week 24, based on Long COVID/PASC PRO between ibuzatrelvir and placebo in non-hospitalized adult and adolescent patients with symptomatic COVID-19 who are at high risk of progression to severe disease and were treated ≤5 days after COVID-19 symptom onset, and who were not receiving background SoC treatment for their COVID-19 infection at baseline (ie, initiated at randomization), and as defined by study inclusion/exclusion criteria, included in the mITT Analysis Set. For the intercurrent event of discontinuation from the study, only Long COVID/PASC PROs completed at Week 12 or later will be utilized in the analysis.

endpoints.description: Proportion of participants with Long COVID/PASC symptoms at Week 24, based on the Long COVID/PASC PRO

sources.raw_text: 9.3.2.1. Definition of Endpoint(s) The composite primary endpoint is the proportion of participants with COVID-19-related emergency department visits with administration of supplemental oxygen, COVID-19 antiviral or IV treatment (eg, hydration, antibiotics, or corticosteroids), all-cause hospitalization, or all-cause death through Day 28.

sources.raw_text: 9.3.4. Secondary Endpoint(s)/Estimand(s) Analysis Change from baseline in SARS-CoV-2 RNA in NP/nasal samples over time, as assessed by an MMRM analysis of covariance model, including baseline SARS-CoV- 2 RNA level, vaccination status and symptom duration as covariates, and region, treatment, time (ie, visit), treatment-by-time as fixed effects, will be used. sources.raw_text: 4.1. Overall Design This is a Phase 3, superiority, adaptive, 2-arm, parallel group, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter study in non-hospitalized, symptomatic, adult and adolescent participants with COVID-19 who are at high risk of progressing to severe illness, to determine the efficacy, safety, and tolerability of ibuzatrelvir. Approximately 2330 eligible participants with a confirmed diagnosis of SARS-CoV-2 infection within 1 day prior to randomization and with initial onset of symptoms of COVID-19 within 5 days prior to randomization will be randomized (1:1) to receive ibuzatrelvir or placebo orally twice daily for 5 days (10 doses total). After the 5-day treatment phase, participants will be followed up until Week 24 for safety and efficacy endpoint assessments. Randomization will be stratified by geographic region (North America, Europe or Rest of World) and planned background standard of care (SoC) use at baseline (yes or no). All participants will be permitted to receive the local antiviral SoC treatment for COVID-19 either as planned background SoC or as unplanned rescue medication due to worsening COVID-19 or any other reason. Planned background SoC initiated at baseline on Day 1 (ie, at randomization, and not before) is considered to be acceptable as this is planned to treat the patient's disease (if required), upon entry into the study, prior to any assessment of efficacy, and this represents the best available therapy upon which a treatment benefit for ibuzatrelvir is being demonstrated. Addition of antiviral medication that starts after baseline is considered to be unplanned rescue medication and is handled as an intercurrent event. Worsening COVID-19 is defined as worsening of COVID-19 symptoms that are at least moderate or severe in intensity OR evidence of COVID-19 disease progression as evidenced by physical examination, laboratory tests or imaging. A single planned interim analysis assessing futility (non-binding) with a sample size reassessment will be conducted for the composite primary efficacy endpoint and reviewed by an independent external data monitoring committee (E-DMC) after approximately 70% of participants have completed the Day 34 assessments. The E-DMC will also review safety data on an ongoing basis. An adjudication committee will review whether rescue medication use was due to worsening COVID-19 and adjudicate efficacy endpoints

population.description: Phase 3, superiority, adaptive, 2-arm, parallel group, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter study in non-hospitalized, symptomatic, adult and adolescent participants with COVID-19 who are at high risk of progressing to severe illness.

Approximately 2330 eligible participants with a confirmed diagnosis of SARS-CoV-2 infection within 1 day prior to randomization and with initial onset of symptoms of COVID-19 within 5 days prior to randomization will be randomized (1:1) to receive ibuzatrelvir or placebo orally twice daily for 5 days (10 doses total). 1.1. Synopsis Study Arm(s) Arm Title Treatment arm Placebo arm Arm Description Participants will receive 600 mg Participants will receive 0 mg BID on twice a day (BID) on Day 1 to Day 5 Day 1 to Day 5 (10 doses in total) (10 doses in total)

4.3. Justification for Dose A dosing regimen of 600 mg ibuzatrelvir twice daily administered orally for 5 days will be evaluated in this study. Dose selection for this study included consideration of all relevant available preclinical and clinical data, including repeat-dose toxicology studies, in vitro pharmacology studies, and clinical safety and PK data from Phase 1 and Phase 2b studies of ibuzatrelvir.

4.1. Overall Design The total study duration is up to 24 weeks and includes a screening period up to 1 day before randomization, study intervention administration through Day 5, primary efficacy assessments through Day 28 and safety F/U through Day 34. The F/U will continue monthly from Week 8 through Week 24 with telemedicine visits at Week 8, Week 16, and Week 20 and in person visits at Week 12 and Week 24, for the assessment of secondary efficacy endpoints. The total number of visits from screening to Day 34 is 7. Screening procedures may be completed on the same calendar day as randomization and Baseline/Day 1 procedures, including the first dose of study intervention. Day 21 and Day 34 visits may be performed as telemedicine visits if the participant is asymptomatic.

studyDesigns.description: This is a Phase 3, superiority, adaptive, 2-arm, parallel group, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter study in non-hospitalized, symptomatic, adult and adolescent participants with COVID-19 who are at high risk of progressing to severe illness, to determine the efficacy, safety, and tolerability of ibuzatrelvir. Approximately 2330 eligible participants with a confirmed diagnosis of SARS-CoV-2 infection within 1 day prior to randomization and with initial onset of symptoms of COVID-19 within 5 days prior to randomization will be randomized (1:1) to receive ibuzatrelvir or placebo orally twice daily for 5 days (10 doses total). The total study duration is up to 24 weeks and includes a screening period up to 1 day before randomization, study intervention administration through Day 5, primary efficacy assessments through Day 28 and safety follow-up through Day 34. Follow-up will continue monthly from Week 8 through Week 24, with telemedicine visits at Week 8, Week 16, and Week 20 and in person visits at Week 12 and Week 24, for the assessment of secondary efficacy endpoints. The study design incorporates an interim analysis with possibility for sample size modification. Randomization will be stratified by geographic region (North America, Europe or Rest of World) and planned background standard of care use at baseline (yes or no).

1.1. Synopsis AN INTERVENTIONAL EFFICACY AND SAFETY, PHASE 3, DOUBLE-BLIND, 2-ARM STUDY TO INVESTIGATE ORALLY ADMINISTERED IBUZATRELVIR COMPARED WITH PLACEBO IN NON-HOSPITALIZED SYMPTOMATIC ADULT AND ADOLESCENT PARTICIPANTS WITH COVID-19 WHO ARE AT HIGH RISK OF PROGRESSING TO SEVERE ILLNESS A Study to Learn About a Study Medicine Called Ibuzatrelvir in Adult and Adolescent Patients With Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) Who Are Not Hospitalized But Are at Risk For Severe Disease Regulatory Agency Identification Number(s): US IND Number: 162644 EU Clinical Trial (CT) Number: 2024-517727-39-00 ClinicalTrials.gov ID: NCT06679140 Pediatric Investigational Plan Number: EMA/PE/0000229195 Protocol Number: C5091017 Phase: 3 Ibuzatrelvir is a potent and selective inhibitor of the severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) main protease (Mpro) with low risk for drug-drug interactions (DDIs). The purpose of the study is to evaluate the efficacy and safety of ibuzatrelvir for the treatment of non-hospitalized, symptomatic, adult and adolescent participants with COVID-19 who are at high risk of progressing to severe illness.

sources.raw_text: 1.3. Schedule of Activities The SoA table provides an overview of the protocol visits and procedures. Refer to the STUDY ASSESSMENTS AND PROCEDURES section of the protocol for detailed information on each procedure and assessment required for compliance with the protocol. The investigator may schedule visits (unplanned visits) in addition to those listed in the SoA table, in order to conduct evaluations or assessments required to protect the well-being of the participant. The SoA from screening through the treatment period and follow-up period to Day 34 is provided in Table 1. A continuation of the SoA for the follow-up period from Week 8 through Week 24 is provided in Table 2.

1.3. Schedule of Activities Visit Identifier F/U Notes Abbreviations used in this table may be found in Week (+/- 7 days) Section 10.15 8 12 16 20 24 ET Weeks 8, 16, and 20 are telemedicine visits. Week 12 and 24 are in-person visits. Visit weeks are relative to start of study intervention (Day 1) Qualitative participant interviews X Interviews will be conducted in a subset of participants (approximately 30) once follow- up for Long COVID is completed (Week 24 ± 7 days). See Section 8.10.

sources.raw_text: 5.1. Inclusion Criteria 1. 12 to <18 years of age, weighing at least 40 kg (where permitted by local regulation), or ≥18 years of age of any weight at screening. WOCBP may be enrolled if they agree to use a highly effective method of contraception. Refer to Appendix 4 for reproductive criteria for male (Section 10.4.1) and female (Section 10.4.2) participants.

eligibilityCriteria.name: 2. Presence of risk factors for progression to severe COVID-19 at the time of screening based on age: a. 12 to 49 years of age with at least two risk factors, w
eligibilityCriteria.label: 2. Presence of risk factors for progression to severe COVID-19 at the time of screening based on age: a. 12 to 49 years of age with at least two risk factors, w
eligibilityCriteria.description: 2. Presence of risk factors for progression to severe COVID-19 at the time of screening based on age: a. 12 to 49 years of age with at least two risk factors, where one must be moderate immunocompromise b. 50 to 64 years of age with at least two risk factors; c. 65 to 74 years of age with at least one risk factor; d. For participants 75 years of age or older, there are no requirements related to risk factors. The list of risk factors includes: BMI ≥35 kg/m2; Current smoker (cigarette smoking within the past 30 days) and history of at least 100 lifetime cigarettes; Chronic lung disease (ie, bronchiectasis, cystic fibrosis, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, interstitial lung disease, history of pulmonary embolism, pulmonary hypertension); Asthma, requiring daily prescribed therapy; Cardiovascular disease, defined as history of any of the following: coronary artery disease, cardiomyopathies, myocardial infarction, stroke, transient ischemic attack, heart failure, angina with prescribed nitroglycerin, coronary artery bypass graft, percutaneous coronary intervention, carotid endarterectomy, and aortic bypass; Type 1 or Type 2 diabetes mellitus; Mild to moderate renal impairment provided participant doesn't meet Exclusion Criterion 2; Neurodevelopmental disorders (eg, cerebral palsy, Down's syndrome) or other conditions that confer medical complexity (eg, genetic or metabolic syndromes and severe congenital anomalies), as long as the cognitive function allows consenting and completion of study procedures (eg, diaries); Sickle cell disease; Moderate immunosuppression (see Appendix 13).

sources.raw_text: 10.1.5.1. Data Monitoring Committee This study will use an E-DMC. The E-DMC is independent of the Pfizer study team and includes only external members. The E-DMC charter describes the role of the E-DMC in more detail.

10.1.5.1. Data Monitoring Committee The E-DMC will be responsible for ongoing monitoring of the efficacy and safety of participants in the study according to the charter. The recommendations made by the E-DMC will be forwarded to the appropriate authorized Pfizer personnel for review and final decision. Pfizer will communicate such decisions, which may include summaries of aggregate analyses of endpoint events and of safety data that are not endpoints, to regulatory authorities and investigators, as appropriate.
